critic_raw:
This experiment comparing RNN vs. logistic regression for hospital readmission using patient visit sequences has several methodological concerns. The stratified random split is justified by the claim that 'temporal autocorrelation effects introduced by hospital practices are weak' — but this is not an empirically supported claim for EHR data. Hospital policies, seasonal admission patterns, and evolving treatment protocols are strongly time-dependent. Random splitting of multi-year EHR data allows future clinical patterns (e.g., a new treatment protocol introduced in year 3) to be seen in training when year-3 observations are split into both train and test. A chronological split by admission date is the methodologically defensible choice.

The patient-level isolation is not addressed: the same patient may have multiple admissions in the 10,000+ visit dataset across three years. If one admission from a patient is in training and another in test, the model may recognize patient-specific clinical signatures (e.g., specific diagnosis code patterns unique to that patient's chronic condition trajectory), effectively leaking patient identity across the split. This inflates RNN performance because the RNN is better positioned to memorize patient-specific sequence patterns than logistic regression.

The comparison gives the RNN access to 'temporally encoded visit sequences and additional demographic and diagnostic attributes' while logistic regression uses 'demographic and clinical features' — the feature sets are not described as equivalent. If logistic regression does not receive sequence-derived features (e.g., number of prior admissions, time since last visit, trend in lab values), the comparison is unfair, attributing gains to the model architecture when features drive the difference.

AUROC is the primary metric, but for a readmission intervention program, calibration and net benefit are more clinically relevant. The mention of 'stratified by hospital' as a confound control is positive, but whether the split is performed within hospitals or across hospitals (leave-hospital-out evaluation) is not specified — the former merely controls for hospital effects within the split, while the latter tests generalization to new hospitals.
all_issues_raised: Stratified random split on multi-year EHR data ignores temporal autocorrelation from evolving hospital policies and seasonal patterns, potentially inflating generalization estimates.

Patient-level leakage: multiple admissions from the same patient distributed across train and test allow the RNN to memorize patient-specific sequence patterns rather than generalizable clinical signals.
Feature set asymmetry: RNN receives richer temporal sequence features while logistic regression uses aggregate demographic and clinical variables — architecture and feature richness are confounded.
Calibration is not mentioned — for clinical readmission tools used to prioritize interventions, probability calibration is as important as rank-ordering (AUROC).
Hospital stratification is noted as a confound control but leave-hospital-out evaluation (testing on held-out hospitals) is not considered, leaving generalization to new care settings unverified.
Patient-level containment across splits is not explicitly specified; multi-admission patients may leak visit sequences between training and test
Temporal autocorrelation in hospital readmission risk is asserted as weak without empirical validation
Logistic regression baseline and RNN use different feature sets, conflating feature richness with architecture contribution
AUROC aggregates across thresholds; clinical deployment requires calibration and performance at specific operating points
Stratification by hospital controls for site effects but does not address temporal confounding from policy changes within hospitals over three years
Stratified random split on three-year patient EHR data is not supported by evidence of temporal stationarity; seasonal and secular trends in readmission patterns require chronological splitting

Comparison confounds model class (RNN vs. logistic regression) with feature representation (sequential vs. aggregated); no intermediate baselines to isolate which factor drives performance differences
Patient-level grouping is absent from split strategy — patients with multiple admissions spanning the three-year period could appear in both training and test sets, creating patient-level leakage
Sequence padding strategy is unspecified — padding value, maximum sequence length, and missingness handling within sequences substantially affect RNN performance but are not described
No calibration evaluation is specified despite clinical decision-making requiring well-calibrated probability outputs for readmission risk stratification thresholds
